cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer to this question. The story is about an employer calculating the governess Julia's pay, making various deductions and accusations against her work. The question is asking why the employer made those criticisms. The options are A through D. First, let me recall the story. The employer starts by saying they agreed on thirty rubles a month, but Julia claims forty. He insists on thirty and starts deducting days she didn't work—Sundays, holidays, days the kids were sick, days she had a toothache. Then he adds more deductions for broken items and incidents like the kid climbing a tree and losing shoes. He keeps subtracting money, bringing her pay down. At the end, Julia keeps being silent even when he's clearly taking more than he should. The employer gets angry because she doesn't protest and ends up giving her more money, realizing how spineless she is. The key here is the employer's motivation for the criticisms. Let's look at each option: A) She had neglected her duties. The story mentions some incidents (broken teacup, kid climbing tree, stolen shoes), but these might be used as excuses. However, the employer later admits he played a cruel joke on her and gives her the full 80 rubles, implying that maybe the criticisms weren't entirely justified. So maybe the criticisms were not because of actual neglect. B) He wanted to pay her as little money as possible. This seems possible. He's making all these deductions, maybe exaggerating or inventing reasons to reduce her pay. The employer is described as someone who likes to make notes and stick to his terms, possibly to minimize expenses. C) He wanted to make her realize she was being cheated and protest. This is what he says at the end: he wanted to teach her a lesson because she didn't protest. He says, "I played a little joke on you, a cruel lesson, just to teach you..." So this seems like the direct reason. He wanted her to stand up for herself. D) He wanted to make her feel miserable. While he does make her emotional (she cries, is nervous), the stated purpose isn't just to make her feel bad but to teach her to protest. The answer might not be D. The employer's own words at the end clarify his intention: he wanted to teach her a lesson by being cruel, to show her that she should protest when being cheated. So the answer is C.
